title: Rich out of process low level bindings
body:
For bootc going forward we are primarily writing most code in Rust. This project is in Go and there's a lot of that in the ecosystem, which won't change anytime soon - and that's fine.

We've been using https://github.com/containers/skopeo/blob/main/docs-experimental/skopeo-experimental-image-proxy.1.md for a long time, it works OK. However, it's missing some things like push.

Now podman of course has a HTTP API, but it's both high level and also has a constraint to match docker. For example:

- Can't just fetch an individual layer or manifest
- Can't copy an image to a non-registry destination

etc.

So my strawman here is:

- Add a proxy-like API into this project that is attempting to be much more of an *automated* and comprehensive binding
- Expose that proxy in both podman+skopeo at least (ref https://github.com/containers/skopeo/issues/2576#issuecomment-2798030293 and below

## Options for transport

A ton of choices here, I kind of like JSON-RPC as it's widely used in e.g. MCP and LSP protocols. But we'd need fd passing support.

Or we could try to extend the docker HTTP with extra container-libs specific methods and also negotiate a separate socketpair for fd passing?

comments.body:
>  _automated_ binding

I don’t know how that could be accomplished (and at what binary size cost!) but it sounds interesting.

---

Two general concerns:
- Podman does a _lot_ of defaulting / environment setup which is not provided as an in-process API (and most of that is not designed to be consumed a la carte). Yes using `skopeo` is already missing that part.
- For a lot of things that are generally contemplated / hinted at, c/storage just doesn’t have a stable API at that level. I suppose we need _both_ a stable API and a way to consume it from Rust, so this is not a blocker _per se_, but it is a blocker _for the total effort_.

>    For a lot of things that are generally contemplated / hinted at, c/storage just doesn’t have a stable API at that level. I suppose we need both a stable API and a way to consume it from Rust, so this is not a blocker per se, but it is a blocker for the total effort.

One possibility here is that the calling process vendors its own copy of the Go code as a private binary...but it's obviously much much much nicer if there's a stable API.

It’s not just a matter of stability commitment — it’s also things like making overlay-specific assumptions that wouldn’t work on VFS/btrfs, or using OS-provided file descriptors instead of Go-native `io.Reader`.

> it’s also things like making overlay-specific assumptions that wouldn’t work on VFS/btrfs, 

Is it? I think a generic API for fetching tar-diff + get access to file descriptors for specific paths would work equally well with vfs/btrfs.

> or using OS-provided file descriptors instead of Go-native io.Reader.

Hmm didn't we drop Windows support in https://github.com/containers/container-libs/commit/15f75b773f7f72773f70808106a9627b4354f9ff ? So that just (effectively) leaves Unix systems which I guess gets into *BSD support questions (or OK if one gets very theroetical, Redox etc.) but we're just talking about very very standard unix stuff here.

> > it’s also things like making overlay-specific assumptions that wouldn’t work on VFS/btrfs,
> 
> Is it? I think a generic API for fetching tar-diff + get access to file descriptors for specific paths would work equally well with vfs/btrfs.

c/storage has not been externally promising to store / provide tar-split; that’s been an implementation detail. On snapshot-based file systems like btrfs, a layer must be mounted (and unmounted!) in order for a file descriptor to be available.

> > or using OS-provided file descriptors instead of Go-native io.Reader.
> 
> we're just talking about very very standard unix stuff here.

Sure, it’s _possible_ but it doesn’t exist as an API because the Go callers prefer an `io.Reader`.
